assessor_1_raw: The site-camera-label correlation is the dominant issue. The data structure effectively labels camera hardware as a risk signal: newer polarized cameras appear exclusively at benign-dominant suburban clinics; older cameras appear at the academic melanoma-dominant center. The reported mitigations — color augmentation and resolution standardization — address incidental within-image variation, not the systematic between-camera color response difference that is consistent across the academic center's images. The model trained on this data can discriminate high-from-low risk using image quality and color statistics as proxies, without learning lesion morphology.
assessor_2_raw: The prospective evaluation was conducted during a period when the academic center expanded its biopsy triage clinic, changing the case mix in the rollout set. The training set was collected over nine months prior, when this expansion had not yet occurred. The rollout set therefore has a different positive prevalence at the site that was already the primary melanoma contributor. A 7-point AUROC gain measured on a higher-prevalence rollout set at the camera-confounded site may not replicate on a clinically representative evaluation set.
assessor_3_raw: The absence of site-stratified evaluation is decisive. Without it, there is no way to determine whether the 7-point improvement is driven by genuine model improvement across all sites or by a shift in the academic center's contribution to the pooled evaluation. The authors acknowledge this gap ('no site-stratified results') but draw deployment conclusions from pooled figures. This is an unsupported generalization.
synthesis_raw:
SYNTHESIS — hidden_confounding_skin_sites_205 — Ensemble Run 2

Consistent pattern across three independent assessors. Camera-site-label confound, rollout base rate shift, and missing site-stratified analysis are all identified. No disagreement across assessors.

Verdict: CRITIQUE_WINS
